source_texts: “What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”

All six tiny little rooms were steam heated, as a result of which you were suffocated by moist heat in the apartment. The window panes sweated; the visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofia Petrovna Likhutina herself was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum. How could there possibly be any perspective?
He stood there a moment, took several deep breaths, shook his head, turned around, and did not notice Semyonych† flattened against the wall in a shadowy corner of the corridor.
In the vestibule Nikolai Apollonovich stopped in front of the lackey, trying to catch a thought that had scampered off.

The wonderful thing about the collision between young minds is that you can never predict the spark or anticipate the flash. What will the next moment bring? No one knows. The burst of laughter succeeds compassion. In the comic instant seriousness comes into play. Impulses are prompted by a chance word. Each individual’s verve is paramount. A joke is enough to create an opening for the unexpected. These are exchanges that take abrupt turns, with sudden realignments of perspective. Fortuitousness is the scene-shifter in such conversations.
The tiny figure stopped, and imploringly stretched out toward the phosphorescent intervals between the branches, which were forming the body.

That comes, I think, from their tiredness. Over the centuries they have taken upon themselves the entire task of love; they have always played the whole dialogue—both parts. For man has only repeated their words, and done it badly. And has made their learning difficult with his distractedness, his negligence, his jealousy, which was itself a kind of negligence.

“I’ve been a widow for two years,” she said in a half whisper, shuddering as she spoke. “It was hard being married to him, he was old and beat me terribly. So when he lay sick, I looked at him and I said to myself: ‘If he gets better, he’ll get up and then what?’ And that is when the thought came to me . . .”
“Wait, you didn’t get that from me!” Ivan suddenly stopped, struck by this fact. “It never even occurred to me . . . Strange!”

“Well, I’d rather put it this way: you and I, we have the same philosophy. That would be a much fairer statement. Je pense, donc je suis, that is something I know for certain. As for the rest—all those worlds, God, and even Satan himself—I’m not sure whether all that exists independently or is merely a passing and subjective emanation of myself .
rationales: Outputs 0 and 3 share several clear stylistic and structural similarities. Both use quotation marks and keep very close to the source in terms of phrasing: 'What, the priest?', 'I have no sins', 'God must/should forgive me without that', 'He knows how I have suffered', and very similar closing sentiments ('If He won’t forgive me, I don’t care!' vs 'if He doesn’t forgive me, then so be it!'). The ordering and directness of their sentences are much closer to each other than to the other paraphrases, and both retain a question as the opening. The slight variations between the two (e.g. 'then so be it!' vs 'I don’t care!') are minimal, suggesting the same paraphrasing system with some degree of random variation.

Outputs 1 and 4 both mention the 'apartment's six small rooms' and focus heavily on the atmosphere being thick, humid, and uncomfortable. Both mention the condensation and sweating in a narrative sequence ('everyone present - the visitors, the maid, Sofia's husband, and even Sofia herself'), and both conclude with a statement that connects the physical discomfort to the loss of perspective or clarity, using similar structures ('eliminate any sense of calm or hope', 'any sense of perspective or clarity seemed utterly lost'). The specific way these are described (air described as 'thick and uncomfortable with moisture', rooms being 'steam-heated', and the sequence of people mentioned) indicates a similar paraphraser underlying both outputs.

Outputs 1 and 2 share highly similar structure and wording. Both start with a brief pause, mention inhaling deeply a few times, shaking his head, spinning around, and both describe Semyonych pressed against the wall in a 'dimly lit part of the hallway.' The sequence of actions, language choices ('inhaled deeply a few times', 'spun around', 'pressed against the wall in a dimly lit part'), and omission of the latter half of the scene found in output 0 all strongly suggest they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 both use the phrase 'paused in the ...' followed by a location ('entryway' for 2 and 'vestibule' for 3), and both focus on Nikolai Apollonovich trying to grasp or recapture a 'fleeting thought/idea.' Their sentence structure and diction are notably similar: both start with Nikolai's action (paused in...), followed by his position (location before/without reference to the servant), and end with the mental action (attempting/struggling to grasp/recapture a fleeting thought/idea). These shared stylistic elements and patterns suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Paraphrases 0 and 4 share notable stylistic and structural similarities. Both use contemporary, conversational language, focusing on unpredictability, laughter leading to insights, the role of chance, and unexpected conversational turns. Both also describe how humor can open up more genuine interaction and specifically mention dynamics and insights emerging. Their sentences are structured similarly, with smooth transitions and a narrative flow that isn't as abrupt or poetic as in 2 and 3. In contrast, 2 and 3 use more archaic or literary phrasing ('verve of each is sovereign', 'the collision between young minds', etc.), further separating them from 0 and 4.

Outputs 1 and 3 both describe the figure as 'small' and use similar structure and imagery—mentioning arms extending upwards in a yearning or desperate gesture, and describing light/glowing spaces between branches taking shape or forming a form. Both lean into more evocative language ('yearning towards', 'desperately extending', 'taking shape into a coherent form'), suggesting a similar paraphrasing style and lexical choices.

Indexes 1 and 2 both use very similar phrasing and sentence structure, suggesting they are from the same paraphraser. Both open with remarks about weariness ('That comes, I think, from their tiredness.' and 'It comes, I believe, of their weariness.'), and both share the unique phrase 'played the whole dialogue both parts / played the entire dialogue, both parts.' Both also use the construction 'man has only repeated their words, and done it badly' or very close variants. The pattern of listing faults with 'his absent-mindedness, his negligence, his jealousy, which was itself a kind of negligence,' is similarly echoed in both versions. These consistent linguistic patterns and unique expressions are not present in the other paraphrases.

Indexes 3 and 4 share highly similar phrasing, sentence structure, and even direct use of quoted speech with the same pacing and turns of phrase. The half-whisper, the shuddering, the chronological narration ('he was old and beat me', 'when he was sick I looked at him and said to myself', 'the thought came to me') are nearly identical, suggesting they are from the same paraphraser. Other paraphrases are distinct in summary style, narration, or internal voice usage.

Outputs 0 and 4 are nearly identical in structure, wording, and punctuation. Both start with 'Wait, you didn’t get (that/it) from me!' and then describe Ivan stopping suddenly, being struck by the fact, and concluding with a nearly identical quote about the idea never occurring to him. Minor differences such as 'that'/'it' do not significantly distinguish them, indicating they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Indexes 3 and 4 both preserve a longer, more elaborate sentence structure, and include similar phrases such as 'if you like, I have/am of the same philosophy as you,' 'Je pense, donc je suis,' and the elaboration about all the worlds, God, and even Satan being 'unproven' or 'not proved.' Both also contain a concluding statement about breaking off or making haste to stop, fearing the listener's reaction, which is absent or much less prominent in the other outputs. Their language structure is distinctly closer to the source and to each other than to the rest.

human_par3: ‘What’s that? A priest?… Don’t bother… Where are you going to find a spare rouble? … I’ve no sins on me!… God will have to forgive me without that… He knows how I’ve suffered!… And if he won’t forgive me, too bad!…’
“What, the priest? I don’t want him. You haven’t got a ruble to spare. I have no sins. God must forgive me without that. He knows how I have suffered . . . And if He won’t forgive me, I don’t care!”

human_par3: "Well, if you like, I have the same philosophy as you, that would be true. _Je pense, donc je suis_, I know that for a fact; all the rest, all these worlds, God and even Satan--all that is not proved, to my mind. Does all that exist of itself, or is it only an emanation of myself, a logical development of my ego which alone has existed for ever: but I make haste to stop, for I believe you will be jumping up to beat me directly."

‘Well, if you like, I am of the same philosophy as you, now that it would be true to say. Je pense, donc je suis , that is the one thing I know for sure, and as for all the rest of what surrounds me, all these worlds, God and even Satan himself – for me it remains unproven whether all that exists in itself or is merely a certain emanation of mine, the logical development of my I, which has a pre-temporal and individual existence … in a word, I break off in haste, for you look as though you are about to jump up and box my ears.’

Je pense, donc je suis , that is the one thing I know for sure, and as for all the rest of what surrounds me, all these worlds, God and even Satan himself – for me it remains unproven whether all that exists in itself or is merely a certain emanation of mine, the logical development of my I, which has a pre-temporal and individual existence … in a word, I break off in haste, for you look as though you are about to jump up and box my ears.’

‘What’s that? A priest?… Don’t bother… Where are you going to find a spare rouble? … I’ve no sins on me!… God will have to forgive me without that… He knows how I’ve suffered!… And if he won’t forgive me, too bad!…’

The windows drooped with condensation, and everyone present - the visitors, the maid, Sofia's husband, and even Sofia herself - was suffocating under the humid heat, their bodies glistening with sweat. It was no wonder then that any sense of perspective or clarity seemed utterly lost amidst the sweltering atmosphere.

When young people interact, the resulting dynamics are always unpredictable; you never know what unexpected ideas or insights will emerge. What happens next is a complete mystery. Laughter can follow empathy, and a serious thought can arise from something humorous. A single word can trigger new impulses. Each person's enthusiasm is key. Even a simple joke can open the door to something entirely unforeseen. These conversations often shift direction abruptly, leading to sudden changes in how people see things. Chance plays a major role in how these discussions unfold.

Throughout centuries, women have borne the entirety of love's responsibilities. They've essentially played both roles, taking on the entire emotional dialogue. Meanwhile, men have simply mimicked their words, often with poor results. Moreover, men have complicated women's emotional journeys with their own thoughtless actions, such as jealousy, which can be seen as a form of abandonment or neglect.

"I am a widow these three years," she began in a half-whisper, with a sort of shudder. "I had a hard life with my husband. He was an old man. He used to beat me cruelly. He lay ill; I thought looking at him, if he were to get well, if he were to get up again, what then? And then the thought came to me--"
“Wait, you didn’t get it from me!” Ivan suddenly stopped, struck by this fact. “It never even occurred to me... Strange!”

"Well, if you like, I have the same philosophy as you, that would be true. _Je pense, donc je suis_, I know that for a fact; all the rest, all these worlds, God and even Satan--all that is not proved, to my mind. Does all that exist of itself, or is it only an emanation of myself, a logical development of my ego which alone has existed for ever: but I make haste to stop, for I believe you will be jumping up to beat me directly."
